title: Testing and debugging code at the level of individual functions and methods
outline: The function/method as the smallest testable unit of a program.
Unit testing: arrange–act–assert, test cases and edge cases.
Combining unit tests with the debugger to localize failures.
Test doubles: stubs, mocks and fakes for isolating a method.
From a failing test to a fixed method: the practical workflow.
body: A function or method is the smallest self-contained unit of behaviour in a program: it receives arguments, performs a computation and returns a result or produces a side effect. Because it has a clearly defined contract — its inputs, its output and its expected effects — a function is the most convenient granularity at which to test and debug. If every function behaves correctly in isolation, most defects in the larger program reduce to errors of composition rather than errors of logic.

Testing at the level of a single function is called unit testing. A unit test is itself a small piece of code that calls the function with known inputs and checks that the returned value or observed effect matches an expected value. Modern languages provide dedicated frameworks for this: pytest and unittest in Python, JUnit in Java, NUnit and xUnit in C#, and Google Test in C++. The framework runs the tests, reports which passed and which failed, and shows the difference between expected and actual results.

A well-formed unit test usually follows the arrange–act–assert pattern. In the arrange step the test prepares the inputs and any required context; in the act step it calls the function under test exactly once; in the assert step it compares the result with the expectation. For example, a test of a function add(a, b) arranges the values 2 and 3, acts by calling add(2, 3), and asserts that the result equals 5. Keeping these three steps distinct makes each test easy to read and easy to diagnose when it fails.

The value of unit testing depends heavily on the choice of test cases. A single 'happy path' test that uses typical inputs is not enough. The tester must also cover boundary values and edge cases: empty collections, zero, negative numbers, the maximum and minimum of a range, null or None references, and inputs that should trigger an exception. A function that computes an average, for instance, must be tested with a normal list, with a single element, and with an empty list where division by the length would fail.

Unit tests and the debugger are complementary tools, not alternatives. A test tells you that a function is wrong and, through its name and assertion message, roughly where; the debugger tells you why. The disciplined workflow is: write or run a test that fails, then attach the debugger to that specific test, set a breakpoint inside the function, and step through it while the failing inputs are still fresh. Because a unit test reproduces the defect deterministically and in seconds, it is far more efficient to debug than a full application run.

Most IDEs let you run a single test under the debugger directly. In Visual Studio and VS Code you can right-click a test and choose 'Debug Test'; in IntelliJ IDEA and PyCharm a green arrow in the gutter offers 'Debug'. The IDE stops at the first breakpoint with the exact arguments the test supplied, so you observe the internal state that produced the wrong answer instead of guessing at it from log output.

To test a function in true isolation, its external dependencies must be replaced by controllable substitutes called test doubles. A stub returns a fixed, canned value so the function under test always sees the same input from its collaborator. A mock additionally records how it was called and lets the test assert that the expected calls happened. A fake is a lightweight working implementation, such as an in-memory database used instead of a real one. Libraries such as unittest.mock in Python, Mockito in Java and Moq in C# create these doubles.

Isolation matters because a failing test should point at a single suspect. If a method that computes a price also calls a live currency-conversion service, a failing test could mean the pricing logic is wrong or that the network is down. By replacing the service with a stub that returns a fixed exchange rate, the test exercises only the pricing arithmetic, so any failure unambiguously implicates that arithmetic.

A practical debugging session begins with reproducing the defect as a failing unit test. Suppose users report that a discount method returns a negative price for large orders. The developer first writes a test discount(1000) that asserts a non-negative result; the test fails, capturing the bug permanently. This is close to the idea of test-driven bug fixing: the red test defines 'done' and prevents the same defect from silently returning later.

With the failing test in place, the developer sets a breakpoint on the first line of the method and starts the debugger. Stepping over statements one at a time, they inspect the intermediate values of the discount rate and the running total in the variables window. The moment a value becomes implausible — for instance a discount rate greater than one — marks the neighbourhood of the fault, narrowing a large method down to a single suspect expression.

Once the faulty expression is found, the fix is applied and the test suite is run again. The previously red test must now pass, and — equally important — no previously passing test may turn red. A fix that repairs one case while breaking another is a regression, and the existing tests are precisely the safety net that catches it. This is why a fix is never considered complete until the whole relevant suite is green.

Good unit tests are fast, independent and repeatable. Fast tests, running in milliseconds, encourage developers to run them constantly. Independent tests do not depend on the order in which they run or on shared mutable state, so a failure names exactly one problem. Repeatable tests give the same result every time regardless of the machine, the clock or the network; a test that depends on the current date or a random seed must fix those values explicitly.

Measuring which lines of a function the tests actually execute is the job of code coverage tools such as coverage.py, JaCoCo and the coverage window in Visual Studio. Coverage highlights untested branches — for example an else path that no test ever takes — which are exactly the places where undetected defects tend to hide. Coverage is a guide, not a goal: high coverage of trivial assertions is worth less than modest coverage of carefully chosen edge cases.

It is worth remembering that unit tests verify behaviour against the developer's own expectations, so they cannot reveal a misunderstanding of the requirements. A method may pass every test and still compute the wrong thing because the specification itself was misread. For this reason unit testing at the function level is combined with higher levels of testing — integration and system testing — and with human review of the requirements.

Thus, testing and debugging at the level of individual functions form the foundation of software quality: a unit test pins down a defect as a fast, repeatable, isolated failure, the debugger explains the cause of that failure by exposing internal state, and the surviving green test suite guarantees that the fix neither breaks existing behaviour nor allows the defect to return unnoticed.
questions: Why is a function or method considered the most convenient unit at which to test and debug a program?
Describe the arrange–act–assert pattern and explain why edge cases must be included among the test cases.
How do a unit test and the debugger complement each other when localizing a defect?
What is the difference between a stub, a mock and a fake, and why is isolation important when testing a single method?
What properties make unit tests reliable, and why is code coverage a guide rather than a goal in itself?

title: Working with logs and event journals to track errors
outline: The purpose of logging and its relation to interactive debugging.
Severity levels and the anatomy of a good log record.
Logging frameworks and structured (machine-readable) logs.
Operating-system and application event journals.
Centralized log aggregation and correlation of distributed events.
body: Logging is the practice of recording, during the execution of a program, a stream of messages that describe what the program is doing. Unlike interactive debugging, which pauses a running process to inspect it, logging leaves a permanent written trace that can be read after the fact. This makes logs the primary tool for diagnosing failures that occur on a user's machine or a production server, where attaching a debugger is impossible or forbidden.

A log record should answer the questions of when, where, what and how serious. A useful record therefore contains a timestamp, the source of the message (module, class or function), a severity level, a human-readable description, and enough context — identifiers, parameter values, an error code — to reconstruct the situation. A message such as 'error occurred' is nearly worthless; a message such as '2026-07-02 10:14:03 ERROR order_service.charge order_id=8471 declined: insufficient funds' can be acted upon immediately.

Log messages are classified by severity level so that they can be filtered. The conventional hierarchy, shared by most frameworks, is TRACE and DEBUG for detailed developer diagnostics, INFO for normal significant events, WARNING for suspicious but non-fatal situations, ERROR for failures of a single operation, and CRITICAL or FATAL for failures that threaten the whole application. In production the level is usually set to INFO or WARNING to limit volume, and lowered to DEBUG temporarily when a problem is being investigated.

Choosing the right level for each message is a skill. Over-logging at ERROR level for routine, recoverable events causes 'alert fatigue', where operators learn to ignore errors; under-logging leaves gaps precisely where a failure occurred. A rule of thumb is that an ERROR should represent something a human must eventually look at, a WARNING something the system handled but that may indicate a developing problem, and INFO the milestones of normal operation.

Rather than writing to files with ad-hoc print statements, professional code uses a logging framework: the logging module in Python, Log4j or SLF4J/Logback in Java, Serilog or Microsoft.Extensions.Logging in .NET, and spdlog in C++. These frameworks let the developer emit messages once and then control, through configuration, where the messages go (console, file, network), which levels are recorded, and how records are formatted — all without changing the source code.

A key concept provided by frameworks is the appender or handler, which routes records to a destination, together with rotation policies. Log rotation closes the current file when it reaches a size or age limit and starts a new one, deleting or archiving old files so that logs never fill the disk. A typical policy keeps, for example, the last seven daily files or the last ten files of ten megabytes each.

Traditional logs are lines of free text, easy for a human to read but hard for a machine to search. Structured logging solves this by emitting each record as a set of key–value pairs, usually serialized as JSON: {"ts":"2026-07-02T10:14:03Z","level":"ERROR","svc":"order","order_id":8471,"msg":"charge declined"}. Structured records can be queried precisely — 'show all ERROR records for order_id 8471' — which is essential once the volume of logs exceeds what a person can scan by eye.

A powerful technique in structured logging is the correlation identifier: a unique request ID generated when a request enters the system and attached to every log record produced while handling it. When a user reports a failure at a given time, filtering by their request ID reassembles the complete story of that one request across many functions and even many services, turning a haystack of interleaved messages into a single readable thread.

Operating systems maintain their own event journals independent of the application. On Windows the Event Log, viewed through Event Viewer, records application, security and system events; the .NET EventLog class and PowerShell's Get-WinEvent read and write it. On Linux the systemd journal, queried with journalctl, and the older syslog files under /var/log collect messages from services and the kernel. These journals are indispensable when the failure lies below the application — a service that will not start, a driver error, an out-of-memory kill.

When investigating an incident, the analyst correlates application logs with these system journals by timestamp. An application ERROR at 10:14:03 that coincides with a Windows System-log entry about a disk I/O failure, or a Linux journal entry about the out-of-memory killer terminating the process, immediately reveals a cause that the application log alone could never show. Reading logs is therefore as much about aligning several sources in time as about reading any one of them.

In systems built from many processes and servers, logs are scattered across dozens of machines, so they are shipped to a central store. Aggregation stacks such as the ELK/Elastic stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), Grafana Loki, and cloud services like AWS CloudWatch or Azure Monitor collect, index and visualize logs from the whole system in one searchable place. The developer then queries and builds dashboards across all sources instead of logging into each server.

Logging carries responsibilities beyond diagnostics. Sensitive data — passwords, full card numbers, personal information, authentication tokens — must never be written to logs, both to protect users and to comply with regulations; such fields are masked or omitted. Logging also has a performance cost, because writing to disk or network takes time, so high-volume DEBUG logging is disabled in production and heavy log operations are performed asynchronously to avoid blocking the main work.

Logs work best together with the other observability signals: metrics, which are numeric time series such as request rate and error count, and traces, which follow a single request across services. Logs explain individual events in detail, metrics reveal trends and thresholds, and traces show causal paths; a mature diagnostic practice uses all three, moving from a metric that shows a spike, to the traces of the affected requests, to the exact log records that describe each failure.

Finally, logging must be designed while the code is being written, not bolted on after a failure. Placing informative log statements at the boundaries of important operations — the start and end of a transaction, every caught exception, every external call — costs little at development time and repays itself many times over when a problem must be diagnosed remotely, long after the code was written and with no debugger available.

Thus, logs and event journals extend debugging beyond the developer's machine: severity levels and structured records make failures searchable, correlation identifiers reconstruct the path of a single request, operating-system journals expose faults beneath the application, and centralized aggregation turns the scattered evidence of a distributed system into a single, queryable account of what went wrong.
questions: How does logging differ from interactive debugging, and in what situations is logging the only practical option?
List the common severity levels and explain the consequences of choosing them incorrectly.
What advantages does structured logging with correlation identifiers offer over plain free-text logs?
Why must application logs be correlated with operating-system event journals when investigating an incident?
What precautions regarding sensitive data and performance must be observed when logging in production?

title: Identifying and fixing errors in code
objective: To form practical skills in reproducing a reported defect, localizing its root cause with the debugger and logs, and applying and verifying a correct fix.
theory: Fixing a defect is a disciplined process, not guesswork. The recognized sequence is: reproduce the failure reliably, localize the faulty code, identify the root cause, apply a fix, and verify that the fix works and breaks nothing else. Skipping reproduction leads to 'fixing' code that was never broken; skipping verification lets regressions slip through. A defect that cannot be reproduced cannot be reliably fixed, so establishing a minimal, deterministic reproduction is always the first step.

It is essential to distinguish the symptom from the root cause. A crash reported on line 80 is often only the place where a bad value finally caused damage; the value may have been computed incorrectly dozens of lines earlier. Techniques such as reading the exception stack trace bottom-up, bisecting the input, and moving breakpoints backward from the point of failure help trace a symptom to its origin. A fix applied to the symptom rather than the cause typically produces a new, subtler bug.

Two complementary tactics drive localization. Interactive debugging pauses execution to inspect live state and is ideal when the failure is reproducible locally. Log analysis reads the after-the-fact trace and is ideal when the failure happened elsewhere or intermittently. In practice a developer narrows the region with logs, then confirms the exact cause with the debugger and a targeted breakpoint.
materials: A configured IDE with an integrated debugger (Visual Studio Code, Visual Studio, PyCharm or IntelliJ IDEA).
A prepared small program containing several planted defects (an off-by-one error, a null/None dereference, an incorrect condition, an unhandled exception).
A unit-testing framework for the chosen language (pytest, JUnit, NUnit).
A version-control client (git) for isolating changes and reverting bad fixes.
A defect-report / bug-tracking form or template.
procedure: Read the defect report and reproduce the failure exactly, recording the precise inputs, steps and observed versus expected behaviour; write a failing unit test that captures the defect deterministically.
Read the error message and stack trace from the bottom up to find the originating call, and mark the region of code where the wrong behaviour must arise.
Set a breakpoint in that region, start the debugger, and step through the code while watching the relevant variables until an intermediate value first becomes incorrect.
Form a single hypothesis about the root cause, then confirm or reject it by inspecting state or adding a temporary log statement; iterate until the cause is proven, not guessed.
Apply the smallest correct fix that addresses the root cause, then run the previously failing test to confirm it now passes and run the full suite to confirm no regressions.
Commit the fix together with its test using a clear message that references the defect, and record in the report the cause, the fix and how it was verified.
tasks: Given a function with an off-by-one error in a loop, reproduce the wrong output with a failing test, localize the faulty boundary with the debugger, fix it, and show that the test now passes.

Given code that throws a null/None dereference under certain inputs, use the stack trace and a breakpoint to find where the null value originates rather than where it crashes, and apply a fix at the origin.

Given a function with an incorrect boolean condition that returns wrong results only for boundary inputs, write edge-case tests, localize the faulty condition, correct it, and confirm the whole suite stays green.
conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has formed the skills of reproducing a defect as a failing test, tracing a symptom to its root cause with the debugger and logs, and applying a minimal fix that is verified against the full test suite.
questions: What are the ordered stages of fixing a defect, and why must reproduction come before any change to the code?
How do you distinguish the symptom of a bug from its root cause, and why is fixing the symptom dangerous?
Why is writing a failing unit test a good first step when starting to fix a reported defect?
When is interactive debugging preferable to log analysis for localizing a fault, and when is the reverse true?
Why must the full test suite be run after a fix, and not only the test that originally failed?

title: Handling exceptions and errors in debug mode
outline: Exceptions as a mechanism for signalling and propagating errors.
try/catch/finally, the call stack and stack unwinding.
Debugger control over exceptions: first-chance and second-chance stops.
Reading stack traces and inspecting the exception object.
Anti-patterns and good practice in exception handling.
body: An exception is an object that represents an error or an abnormal condition arising during execution. Instead of returning a special error value that a caller might ignore, the code that detects the problem 'throws' or 'raises' an exception, interrupting the normal flow. Control then jumps to the nearest enclosing handler that is prepared to deal with that kind of error. This mechanism cleanly separates the detection of an error from its handling, which may occur several levels up the call chain.

The handling construct is the try/catch block (called try/except in Python). Code that might fail is placed in the try section; one or more catch/except clauses specify which exception types they handle and what to do about them. An optional finally block runs whether or not an exception occurred and is used to release resources such as files, network connections or locks. Many languages also offer resource-scoped forms — with in Python, using in C#, try-with-resources in Java — that guarantee cleanup automatically.

When an exception is thrown and not handled in the current function, the runtime searches outward through the chain of callers for a matching handler; this process is called stack unwinding. As each frame is abandoned, its local variables are destroyed and any finally blocks it contains are executed. Understanding unwinding is essential in the debugger, because it explains why execution seems to 'jump' from deep inside a function straight to a catch block far above it.

If no handler is found anywhere on the stack, the exception is unhandled and terminates the program, usually printing a stack trace. Handling exceptions in debug mode is largely about catching them at the right moment during this search, before the information about the original failure site is lost. The debugger gives fine control over exactly when execution should pause relative to the throw.

Debuggers distinguish between a first-chance exception and a second-chance exception. A first-chance notification fires the instant the exception is thrown, before the runtime looks for a handler; a second-chance (unhandled) notification fires only if no handler was found. Visual Studio's Exception Settings dialog, for example, lets you choose to break on first-chance for specific exception types. Breaking first-chance is invaluable when an exception is being silently swallowed by an over-broad catch, because it stops you at the throw site even though the program would otherwise continue.

Every major debugger offers a 'break on thrown exceptions' feature. In VS Code you tick 'Uncaught Exceptions' and optionally 'Caught Exceptions' in the Breakpoints panel; in PyCharm and IntelliJ you configure an Exception Breakpoint; in GDB you type 'catch throw' to stop whenever a C++ exception is thrown. These controls let you decide whether to be interrupted at the moment of the throw, the moment it goes unhandled, or both.

When the debugger stops at an exception, the first artefact to read is the stack trace: the ordered list of function calls that were active when the exception was thrown. Read from the top, it shows where the exception originated; read downward, it shows the chain of callers that led there. The trace names files and line numbers, so it points directly at the code to inspect, and it is the single most information-dense clue available at the moment of failure.

The exception object itself carries the details. Its type (for example FileNotFoundError, NullReferenceException, ArithmeticException) classifies the fault; its message describes the specific circumstance; and its data may include an error code or the offending value. Many runtimes chain exceptions: an outer exception caught and re-thrown keeps a reference to the inner 'cause' via InnerException in .NET or the __cause__ attribute in Python. In the debugger you expand the exception object in the variables window to read all of this, including the original cause buried several layers down.

A destructive anti-pattern is the empty or over-broad catch that swallows every exception and does nothing: catch (Exception) { } or a bare except: pass. Such code hides failures completely, so a program continues running with corrupt state and the real error surfaces much later, far from its cause, as an inexplicable symptom. In debug mode this is exactly why first-chance breaking exists — it lets you catch the throw that the code would otherwise silently discard.

Good practice is to catch only the specific exception types you can actually handle, and to catch them at a level where a meaningful recovery or a clear user message is possible. Broad handlers, if used at all, belong at the very top of the program as a last resort that logs the full exception and shuts down gracefully. Catching a narrow type close to the operation keeps unrelated failures propagating to where they can be understood.

When code cannot recover from an error but wants to add context, it should re-throw rather than silently continue. Re-throwing correctly — 'throw;' in C# or 'raise' with no argument in Python — preserves the original stack trace, whereas throwing a new exception without chaining the cause erases the trail back to the origin. Preserving the trace is precisely what makes later debugging possible, so the rule is to wrap-and-chain, never to replace-and-lose.

Exceptions should signal exceptional conditions, not ordinary control flow. Using exceptions to implement a normal loop exit, for instance, is both slow — throwing and unwinding are expensive — and misleading, because it fills the debugger and the logs with 'errors' that are really routine. Expected, frequent situations such as 'item not found' are better represented by return values or option types, reserving exceptions for genuine faults.

A related discipline is validating inputs early and failing fast. Checking arguments at the entry of a function and throwing immediately with a descriptive message turns a defect into an exception at the exact place the bad data arrived, rather than letting it travel deep into the code and cause a confusing failure far away. Fail-fast validation shortens the distance between cause and symptom and thus shortens every future debugging session.

Thus, handling exceptions in debug mode combines language mechanics with debugger control: try/catch/finally and stack unwinding define how errors propagate and how resources are released, first- and second-chance breakpoints let the developer stop exactly when the failure is most informative, and the stack trace together with the exception object reveals both where the error arose and why — provided the code follows the discipline of catching narrowly, chaining causes and failing fast.
questions: What is an exception, and how does stack unwinding deliver it from the throw site to a handler?
What is the role of a finally block, and how do resource-scoped constructs such as 'using' or 'with' relate to it?
Explain the difference between first-chance and second-chance exceptions and when breaking on each is useful.
What information does a stack trace and an exception object provide, and how does exception chaining help debugging?
Why is an empty or over-broad catch block dangerous, and what does correct re-throwing preserve?

title: Debugging multithreaded and asynchronous programs
objective: To form practical skills in reproducing, localizing and fixing concurrency defects such as race conditions and deadlocks using thread-aware debugger features.
theory: Concurrent programs run several flows of execution — threads, tasks or coroutines — that may interleave in an order the developer does not control. This nondeterminism produces defects that are absent in sequential code: a race condition, where the result depends on the timing of unsynchronized access to shared data; a deadlock, where two or more threads each wait forever for a resource the other holds; and starvation, where a thread never gets to run. Because the failing interleaving may occur only rarely, these bugs are notoriously hard to reproduce, earning the name 'heisenbugs' — they can vanish the moment a debugger changes the timing.

Debuggers provide concurrency-specific tools. Visual Studio offers the Parallel Stacks and Threads windows and lets you freeze and thaw individual threads; GDB switches threads with 'thread <n>' and inspects all with 'thread apply all bt'; the JVM and .NET can dump the state of every thread at once (jstack, dotnet-dump). A thread-specific or conditional breakpoint that fires only for one thread avoids the chaos of every thread stopping at a shared line. For a suspected deadlock, a full thread dump showing which lock each thread holds and which it awaits usually reveals the cycle directly.

Asynchronous programs based on async/await or event loops (Python asyncio, JavaScript, C# Task) add their own difficulty: a single logical operation is split across many callbacks or awaited resumptions, so a naive call stack shows only the event-loop machinery, not the logical chain that led there. Modern debuggers reconstruct 'async call stacks' and let you await tasks, while structured concurrency and cancellation tokens help keep asynchronous flows debuggable.
materials: An IDE with concurrency-aware debugging (Visual Studio with Parallel Stacks/Threads, or IntelliJ/PyCharm), or GDB for native code.
A prepared multithreaded program with a planted race condition on a shared counter and a second program with a planted lock-ordering deadlock.
An asynchronous sample using async/await (Python asyncio or C# Task) with an unawaited task defect.
Thread-dump / diagnostic tools for the platform (jstack, dotnet-dump, or the IDE's built-in dump).
Synchronization primitives for the fix (mutex/lock, atomic type, thread-safe queue).
procedure: Run the multithreaded counter program repeatedly and observe that the final total is sometimes wrong, establishing that the defect is a nondeterministic race rather than a logic error.

Open the Threads and Parallel Stacks windows, set a thread-specific breakpoint on the shared update, and observe how two threads read the same value before either writes, confirming the unsynchronized access.
Fix the race by protecting the shared update with a lock or by using an atomic type, then rerun many times to confirm the total is now always correct.
Run the deadlock program until it hangs, capture a full thread dump, and identify the cycle in which each thread holds one lock and waits for the other.
Resolve the deadlock by imposing a consistent global lock-acquisition order, and verify that the program now completes.
In the asynchronous sample, use the debugger's async call stack to find an unawaited task whose exception was lost, add the missing await, and confirm the exception now surfaces and is handled.
tasks: Reproduce and fix a race condition on a shared counter incremented by several threads, demonstrating a correct total before and an incorrect total introduced by removing synchronization.
Diagnose a two-lock deadlock from a thread dump, describe the wait cycle, and eliminate it by enforcing a consistent lock-ordering discipline.
In an async/await program, locate an unawaited task using the debugger, correct it so the awaited exception propagates, and explain why the original bug was silent.
conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has formed the skills of reproducing nondeterministic concurrency defects, using thread- and async-aware debugger features to localize races and deadlocks, and applying synchronization and structured-concurrency fixes that are verified by repeated execution.
questions: What is a race condition, and why does it produce nondeterministic and hard-to-reproduce failures?
How can the Threads and Parallel Stacks windows, or 'thread apply all bt' in GDB, help localize a concurrency bug?
What is a deadlock, and how does a consistent lock-acquisition order prevent it?
Why does a naive call stack fail to show the logical flow of an async/await program, and how do async call stacks help?
Why is a debugger sometimes unable to reproduce a concurrency bug, and what does the term 'heisenbug' describe?

title: Debugging network and distributed systems
outline: Why distributed failures differ from single-process bugs.
Inspecting traffic: packet capture and HTTP-level tools.
The fallacies of distributed computing and partial failure.
Distributed tracing and correlation across services.
Reproducing faults with chaos testing and fault injection.
body: A distributed system is one whose components run in separate processes on separate machines and communicate over a network. Debugging such systems is fundamentally harder than debugging a single program, because there is no single call stack, no single clock and no single place to set a breakpoint. A request may traverse a browser, a load balancer, several services and a database, and the failure may lie in any of them or in the connections between them.

The most direct kind of network problem is that messages do not arrive as expected. To see what is actually on the wire, engineers use packet-capture tools such as Wireshark and tcpdump, which record every packet on an interface and let it be filtered by host, port or protocol. At a higher level, HTTP inspection tools — the browser's DevTools Network tab, the command-line client curl, and API clients like Postman — show requests, responses, headers and status codes for web APIs without the noise of raw packets.

Reading captured traffic answers questions that source code cannot. Is the request being sent at all? Does it reach the server? Is the response a 200, a 404 or a 500? Is a TLS handshake failing? Is a timeout firing because the peer never replied? Many 'application bugs' turn out to be misconfigured addresses, wrong ports, expired certificates or firewall rules, all of which are visible in a packet or HTTP capture but invisible in the code.

Distributed systems fail in ways that single programs do not, summarized by the classic 'fallacies of distributed computing': that the network is reliable, latency is zero, bandwidth is infinite and topology never changes. In reality messages are lost, delayed, duplicated or reordered, and any of these can cause a defect. Robust code must assume the network can fail at any point, and debugging often means proving which of these assumptions was violated.

The defining difficulty is partial failure: in a single process a fault stops everything, but in a distributed system one component can fail while the others keep running, leaving the system in an inconsistent state. A payment may be recorded in one service but not in another because the message between them was lost. Debugging therefore includes reasoning about which side of an interaction completed and how the surviving components should reconcile the difference.

Because there is no shared call stack, the key technique is distributed tracing. A trace assigns a unique trace identifier to a request when it enters the system and propagates that identifier, together with per-hop span identifiers, through every service the request touches. Tools such as OpenTelemetry, Jaeger and Zipkin then assemble these spans into a timeline showing how long each service took and where the request failed. Tracing is, in effect, the distributed replacement for a stack trace.

Correlation ties the whole diagnostic picture together. The same trace identifier is written into every log record produced while handling the request, so filtering the aggregated logs by that identifier reconstructs the complete cross-service story. Without correlation, the logs of a distributed system are an unreadable interleaving of thousands of concurrent requests; with it, one identifier reassembles a single request from beginning to end.

Clock skew complicates the interpretation of distributed evidence. Because each machine has its own clock, ordering events purely by timestamp can be wrong: an effect may appear to precede its cause simply because one server's clock runs a few seconds ahead. Engineers mitigate this by synchronizing clocks with NTP and, in the trace itself, by relying on the causal parent–child relationships of spans rather than on raw wall-clock times.

Timeouts and retries are both a cure and a source of bugs. A caller that waits forever for a dead peer will hang, so calls are given timeouts and are retried on failure. But a retry after a timeout may duplicate an operation that actually succeeded, so operations that can be retried must be idempotent — safe to perform more than once. Many distributed defects are 'retry storms' or duplicated side effects, and diagnosing them requires seeing the retries in the trace and the duplicates in the downstream logs.

The circuit-breaker pattern is a common resilience mechanism whose behaviour must be understood when debugging. After a service has failed repeatedly, the breaker 'opens' and fails fast for a while instead of continuing to call the broken dependency, then periodically probes it. A symptom of 'the service suddenly returns errors instantly' may therefore be an open breaker reacting correctly to an underlying fault, not a new bug — a distinction only visible in the metrics and logs of the breaker itself.

Because distributed faults are timing- and topology-dependent, they are hard to reproduce on demand. Chaos engineering and fault injection deliberately introduce failures — killing a service, adding latency, dropping packets, partitioning the network with tools such as toxiproxy or Chaos Monkey — in a controlled environment. This turns a rare, unreproducible production failure into one that can be triggered at will and studied, which is often the only practical way to debug it.

Effective diagnosis leans on the three pillars of observability working together: metrics reveal that something is wrong and where the load or errors concentrate, traces show the path and timing of the affected requests across services, and logs supply the detailed evidence at each hop. A typical investigation starts from a metric alert, drills into the slow or failing traces, and ends at the specific log lines and, if necessary, a packet capture on the implicated link.

It is also important to reproduce distributed problems in a realistic environment. A bug that appears only under real network latency, real concurrency or real data volume will not show up on a developer laptop where every service runs locally. Staging environments, containerized replicas and traffic replay are used to recreate the conditions under which the distributed failure occurs, because a fault that cannot be reproduced in a representative setting cannot be confidently fixed.

Thus, debugging network and distributed systems replaces the single-process toolset with one built for many independent, unreliable components: packet and HTTP inspection reveal what actually crosses the network, distributed tracing and log correlation reconstruct a request's path in the absence of a shared stack, an awareness of partial failure, clock skew, retries and circuit breakers explains the system's behaviour, and fault injection makes rare failures reproducible enough to diagnose and fix.
questions: Why is there no single call stack or clock to rely on when debugging a distributed system?
What questions can packet-capture and HTTP-inspection tools answer that source code alone cannot?
What is partial failure, and why does it make distributed debugging fundamentally different from single-process debugging?
How do distributed tracing and log correlation together reconstruct the path of a single request across services?
Why must retried operations be idempotent, and how does chaos engineering help in reproducing distributed faults?

title: Performance optimization during the debugging process
outline: Correctness versus performance; when optimization is warranted.
Measure first: avoiding premature and speculative optimization.
Locating bottlenecks: CPU, memory, I/O and contention.
Algorithmic complexity as the dominant lever.
Verifying gains and guarding against regressions.
body: Performance optimization is the process of making a program use fewer resources — time, memory, network or energy — while preserving its correct behaviour. It is closely related to debugging: both are investigations into how a program actually behaves rather than how it was intended to behave, and both rely on evidence gathered from a running system. A performance problem is, in effect, a defect whose symptom is slowness or excessive resource use rather than a wrong result.

The first principle of optimization is that correctness comes before speed. A fast program that produces wrong answers is worthless, so optimization must never break existing behaviour; the test suite that guards correctness is therefore a precondition for any optimization work. Every performance change is validated by re-running the functional tests, because an optimization that quietly changes results is not an optimization but a new bug.

The second and most important principle is to measure before changing anything. Developers are notoriously bad at guessing where a program spends its time, and intuition frequently points at the wrong place. Donald Knuth's famous warning that 'premature optimization is the root of all evil' captures this: optimizing code that is not actually the bottleneck adds complexity and risk for no benefit. The disciplined sequence is measure, identify the bottleneck, optimize it, then measure again.

The tool for measuring where time and memory go is the profiler, treated in depth in the following practical; here it is enough to note that a profiler reports how much of the total runtime each function consumes and how often each is called. Optimization effort is then directed at the small number of functions — often just a handful — that dominate the total, in accordance with the empirical observation that a large fraction of the time is usually spent in a small fraction of the code.

Bottlenecks come in distinct kinds, and identifying the kind determines the cure. A CPU-bound program spends its time computing and is improved by better algorithms or less work; a memory-bound program is limited by allocation, garbage collection or cache misses; an I/O-bound program waits on disk, database or network; and a contention-bound program wastes time on locks in concurrent code. A profiler that shows high wall-clock time but low CPU time, for example, points at I/O or waiting rather than computation.

By far the most powerful optimization is to improve algorithmic complexity. Replacing an O(n^2) algorithm with an O(n log n) or O(n) one yields improvements that grow with the size of the input and dwarf any amount of micro-tuning. A linear search inside a loop that turns an operation into O(n^2) can often be replaced by a hash-based lookup that makes it O(n); choosing the right data structure — a set for membership tests, a dictionary for keyed access, a heap for priorities — is frequently the whole solution.

A recurring performance defect in data-driven applications is the N+1 query problem, in which code fetches a list of items with one query and then issues a separate query for each item, producing hundreds of round trips where one join or batch query would suffice. Such defects are invisible in the source logic but glaring in a profiler or in the database query log, which is why measurement, not reading, reveals them.

Caching trades memory for time by storing the result of an expensive computation or query and reusing it. Memoization of a pure function, an in-process cache of database results, or a distributed cache such as Redis can eliminate repeated work entirely. But caching introduces its own class of bugs — stale data and cache-invalidation errors — so a cache must be added deliberately, with a clear policy for when entries expire, and its correctness verified like any other change.

Micro-optimizations such as reducing allocations in a hot loop, avoiding repeated string concatenation, reusing buffers, or hoisting invariant computations out of a loop are worthwhile only after the algorithmic and structural problems are solved, and only in code the profiler has proven to be hot. Applied blindly across a codebase they harm readability and maintainability while producing negligible gains, which is exactly the premature optimization to avoid.

Concurrency and parallelism can improve throughput by using multiple cores or by overlapping I/O with computation, but they are an optimization of last resort because they introduce the difficult class of concurrency bugs. Parallelizing a CPU-bound computation across cores helps only if the work divides cleanly; overlapping independent I/O requests helps an I/O-bound program regardless of cores. Either way the added complexity must be justified by measured need.

Every optimization must be verified with a repeatable benchmark run under controlled conditions, comparing the before and after on the same inputs and hardware. A single timing is unreliable because of caching, background load and just-in-time warm-up, so benchmarks are repeated and averaged, and warm-up runs are discarded. A claimed speed-up that cannot be demonstrated by a reproducible benchmark is not a real result.

Because a code change can silently make performance worse as well as better, mature projects add performance regression tests to their automated pipeline. These benchmarks fail the build if a key operation becomes slower than an agreed threshold, catching a regression at the moment it is introduced rather than after it reaches users. In this way performance is defended by the same continuous-verification discipline that defends correctness.

Finally, optimization is bounded by clear goals and by the law of diminishing returns. There is no point optimizing an operation that is already fast enough for its purpose, and each successive optimization typically costs more effort for less gain. The engineer sets an explicit target — a response time, a memory budget, a throughput figure — optimizes until it is met, and then stops, because further effort would trade real maintainability for imperceptible speed.

Thus, performance optimization during debugging is a measured, evidence-driven activity that preserves correctness above all: it begins by profiling to locate the true bottleneck, attacks the dominant cost — most often algorithmic complexity or excessive I/O — before any micro-tuning, and confirms every gain with repeatable benchmarks and regression guards so that speed is improved without sacrificing either correctness or maintainability.
questions: Why must correctness and the functional test suite take priority over any performance optimization?
What does 'premature optimization is the root of all evil' mean in terms of the measure-first workflow?
How do CPU-bound, memory-bound, I/O-bound and contention-bound bottlenecks differ, and why does the kind determine the cure?
Why is improving algorithmic complexity usually more valuable than micro-optimization, and what is the N+1 query problem?
How is a performance gain verified reliably, and what is the purpose of performance regression tests?

title: Profiling code to detect bottlenecks
objective: To form practical skills in profiling a program's CPU and memory usage, interpreting the results to locate bottlenecks, and confirming the effect of an optimization with a benchmark.
theory: A profiler is a tool that measures the runtime behaviour of a program: how much time is spent in each function, how many times each is called, and how much memory is allocated. Profilers work in two main ways. A sampling profiler periodically records which function is executing, building a statistical picture with very low overhead; an instrumenting profiler inserts counters at the entry and exit of every function, giving exact call counts and timings at the cost of significant slowdown. Sampling is preferred for realistic performance measurement, instrumenting for precise call analysis.

The output is usually presented as a call graph or a flame graph. A flame graph stacks the call tree so that the width of each box is proportional to the time spent in that function and its children; a wide box near the top is a hot leaf where optimization will pay off. Profilers also distinguish 'self' (or 'exclusive') time, spent in the function itself, from 'total' (or 'inclusive') time, spent in it and everything it calls — a distinction essential to reading results correctly.

Each ecosystem provides mature profilers: cProfile and py-spy for Python, the Visual Studio Performance Profiler and dotnet-trace for .NET, VisualVM and async-profiler for the JVM, and perf and Valgrind's callgrind for native code. Memory profilers such as tracemalloc, dotMemory or Valgrind's massif track allocations and detect leaks. The key discipline in all of them is to profile a realistic workload, because a bottleneck that appears only at production data volumes will not show up on a trivial input.
materials: A CPU profiler for the chosen language (cProfile/py-spy, Visual Studio Profiler, or VisualVM).
A memory profiler (tracemalloc, dotMemory, or Valgrind massif).
A prepared program that is deliberately slow, containing an O(n^2) hot loop and a repeated-allocation defect, together with a representative large dataset.
A benchmarking harness or timing utility for repeatable before/after measurements.
A tool to visualize results (flame-graph viewer or the IDE's built-in call-graph view).
procedure: Run the program on the representative dataset under a CPU profiler and record the total runtime as the baseline.
Sort the profiler output by self time and by call count to identify the one or two functions that dominate the runtime.
Read the call graph or flame graph to confirm whether the cost is in the function itself or in something it calls, and diagnose the bottleneck (for example an O(n^2) loop or an N+1 pattern).

Apply a targeted optimization to the hottest function — such as replacing a linear search with a hash-based lookup or hoisting an allocation out of the loop — while leaving the rest of the code unchanged.
Run the memory profiler to confirm the allocation defect is resolved and that no leak has been introduced.

Re-run the benchmark several times on the same dataset, discard warm-up runs, and compare the averaged after-time with the baseline to quantify the speed-up, confirming that functional tests still pass.
tasks: Profile the provided slow program, identify the single function with the highest self time, and report its share of the total runtime with a screenshot or text extract of the profiler output.
Replace an O(n^2) bottleneck with an O(n) or O(n log n) approach, and present a before/after benchmark table showing runtime on at least two dataset sizes.
Use a memory profiler to locate the repeated-allocation or leak defect, fix it, and show the reduction in total allocations while demonstrating that all functional tests still pass.
conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has formed the skills of profiling CPU and memory usage on a realistic workload, reading call and flame graphs to locate the dominant bottleneck, and confirming an optimization's effect with a repeatable benchmark.
questions: What is the difference between a sampling profiler and an instrumenting profiler, and when is each preferred?
How do 'self' (exclusive) time and 'total' (inclusive) time differ, and why does the distinction matter when reading results?
What does the width of a box in a flame graph represent, and how does that guide optimization effort?
Why must a profiler be run on a realistic, production-scale workload rather than on a trivial input?
Why must a benchmark be repeated with warm-up runs discarded before a speed-up is reported?

title: Testing and debugging code in web applications
outline: The client–server structure of a web application and its two debugging fronts.
Browser DevTools: elements, console, network and sources.
Server-side debugging, logging and API inspection.
The testing pyramid for web applications.
Debugging common web-specific defects: state, CORS and async.
body: A web application is split across two execution environments: client-side code (HTML, CSS and JavaScript) that runs in the user's browser, and server-side code (in languages such as Python, Java, C# or Node.js) that runs on a remote machine and exposes an API. Debugging a web application therefore has two fronts, and the first task when a bug is reported is to decide on which side the fault lies — a decision usually made by inspecting the request and response that cross between them.

The primary client-side tool is the browser's developer tools, opened with F12 in Chrome, Edge or Firefox. DevTools bundles several panels. The Elements (or Inspector) panel shows the live DOM and the CSS rules applied to each node, which is where layout and styling bugs are diagnosed. The Console panel displays JavaScript errors and log output and offers an interactive prompt for evaluating expressions against the live page.

The Network panel is arguably the most important for diagnosing web bugs, because it records every request the page makes — its URL, method, status code, headers, timing and payload. A page that 'does nothing' when a button is clicked can be explained here: perhaps the request returned 404 because the URL is wrong, or 401 because a token expired, or 500 because the server threw an exception. Reading the Network panel is the fastest way to separate a client problem from a server problem.

The Sources (or Debugger) panel is a full JavaScript debugger inside the browser. It lets the developer set breakpoints in client-side code, step through execution, inspect variables and the call stack, and pause on caught or uncaught exceptions — exactly the capabilities of a desktop debugger, applied to code running in the page. Source maps connect minified or transpiled production code back to the original TypeScript or ES modules so that breakpoints land on readable source.

On the server side, the application is debugged with the same tools as any other back-end program: an IDE debugger attached to the running server process, breakpoints in the request handlers, and structured logging of each request and response. Because a web server handles many concurrent requests, correlation identifiers threaded through the logs are essential to isolate the one request that failed from the thousands that succeeded, just as in any distributed system.

Debugging the boundary between client and server is done at the level of the API. Command-line and GUI clients such as curl, HTTPie and Postman send crafted requests directly to an endpoint, removing the browser from the equation so the developer can confirm whether the server behaves correctly in isolation. If curl gets the right answer but the page does not, the fault is on the client; if curl also fails, the fault is on the server.

Testing a web application is organized as a pyramid. At the broad base are many fast unit tests of individual functions and components. In the middle are integration tests that exercise API endpoints against a test database, verifying that the pieces work together. At the narrow top are a few end-to-end tests that drive a real browser through complete user journeys. The pyramid shape reflects cost and speed: unit tests are cheap and fast, end-to-end tests are slow and brittle, so there should be many of the former and few of the latter.

End-to-end browser tests are automated with tools such as Selenium, Cypress and Playwright, which programmatically open a browser, click buttons, fill forms and assert on the resulting page. These tests catch integration failures that unit tests cannot, but they are the flakiest kind of test because they depend on timing, network and rendering. When one fails, tools like Playwright capture a screenshot, a video and a trace at the moment of failure, which are the primary evidence for debugging the failed run.

Certain defects are peculiar to web applications. State-management bugs arise when the data held in the client (in a framework store, in component state, or in the browser's localStorage) drifts out of sync with the server, so the screen shows stale or inconsistent information. These are debugged by inspecting the client state — for example through the React or Vue developer-tools extension — alongside the actual server responses in the Network panel.

A frequently misdiagnosed error is the CORS failure. The browser's same-origin policy blocks a page from reading a response from a different origin unless the server sends the correct Cross-Origin Resource Sharing headers. The symptom is a console message about a blocked request even though the server returned data, and the cure is a server configuration change, not a client one. Recognizing a CORS message saves hours of searching the wrong side of the application.

Asynchronous behaviour is the source of another whole class of web bugs. Because requests complete later and in an unpredictable order, code that assumes data is already present will read undefined values or render before the data arrives; unhandled promise rejections silently swallow errors. These are debugged by pausing on the asynchronous callbacks in the Sources panel, watching the Network timing, and ensuring every promise is awaited and every rejection is handled.

Web applications must also be debugged across a matrix of environments they cannot control: different browsers and versions, different screen sizes, and different network speeds. DevTools can emulate mobile devices and throttle the network to reproduce a bug that appears only on a slow connection or a small screen, and cross-browser testing services run the same tests on many browser–operating-system combinations to catch rendering and compatibility defects.

Security-related failures form a final category that testing must address, because a web application is exposed to the public internet. Input that is not validated or output that is not escaped leads to injection and cross-site-scripting vulnerabilities; these are found through dedicated security testing and code review rather than ordinary functional tests, and the browser console and network capture are used to confirm whether malicious input is reflected unescaped in a response.

Thus, testing and debugging a web application means working on both sides of a client–server boundary: the browser's DevTools expose DOM, console, network and client-side execution, server-side debuggers and correlated logs expose the back-end, API clients isolate the boundary itself, a testing pyramid balances fast unit tests against a few end-to-end journeys, and an awareness of web-specific defects — client state, CORS, asynchrony and security — directs the developer to the correct side of the application quickly.
questions: What are the two execution environments of a web application, and why is deciding which side a bug lies on the first step in debugging?
What is each of the DevTools panels (Elements, Console, Network, Sources) used for when diagnosing a web defect?
How does an API client such as curl or Postman help separate a client-side fault from a server-side fault?
Describe the web testing pyramid and explain why there should be many unit tests but few end-to-end tests.
What is a CORS error, why is it commonly misdiagnosed, and on which side of the application is it fixed?

title: Debugging mobile applications (Android Studio, Xcode)
outline: What makes mobile debugging distinctive: devices, lifecycles and constraints.
Android debugging with Android Studio, ADB and Logcat.
iOS debugging with Xcode, LLDB and Instruments.
Emulators and simulators versus real devices.
Mobile-specific defects: lifecycle, memory, permissions and network.
body: A mobile application runs on a phone or tablet, a device that is fundamentally more constrained and more variable than a desktop: limited memory and battery, a touch interface, an intermittent network, and a strict operating-system lifecycle that can pause or kill the application at any moment. Debugging mobile software therefore combines ordinary code debugging with an understanding of these platform constraints and of the two dominant ecosystems, Android and iOS, each with its own toolchain.

On Android the primary environment is Android Studio, built on IntelliJ IDEA, which provides a full Java/Kotlin debugger with breakpoints, stepping, variable inspection and evaluation of expressions on a running app. The developer connects a device or emulator, launches the app in debug mode, and stops at breakpoints exactly as on the desktop, while the IDE also exposes CPU, memory and network profilers tailored to Android.

The connective tissue of Android debugging is the Android Debug Bridge, ADB, a command-line tool that links the development machine to a device or emulator. Through ADB the developer installs and launches builds, opens a shell on the device, forwards ports, captures screenshots, and — crucially — reads the system log. Commands such as 'adb devices', 'adb install', 'adb logcat' and 'adb shell' are the everyday vocabulary of Android debugging.

Logcat is Android's system-wide logging window. Every app and system component writes tagged, severity-labelled messages to it, and the developer filters by tag, by process or by level to follow their own application's output amid the noise. When an Android app crashes, Logcat shows the exception and its stack trace, which is the first artefact to read; the app's own Log.d/Log.e statements appear here alongside the system messages.

On iOS the environment is Xcode, Apple's IDE, which debugs Swift and Objective-C code using the LLDB debugger. Xcode offers graphical breakpoints, stepping and a variables view, and its debug console accepts LLDB commands directly, so a developer can type expressions such as 'po variable' to print an object or 'bt' to show the backtrace while stopped at a breakpoint. Symbolic breakpoints can stop on a named method even without its source.

Xcode's profiling and diagnostic suite is Instruments, a collection of tools that record a running app's behaviour over time: the Time Profiler for CPU, the Allocations and Leaks instruments for memory, and instruments for energy, network and disk. Because iOS devices are especially sensitive to memory and battery, Instruments is central to finding the resource problems that would cause the operating system to terminate the app or that would drain the battery.

Both platforms let the developer run either a virtual device or a real one. The Android Emulator and the iOS Simulator run the app on the development machine, which is fast and convenient and supports simulating GPS location, incoming calls, low battery and poor network. However, they cannot faithfully reproduce real hardware sensors, camera behaviour, true performance or certain platform quirks, so a bug that appears only on a physical device must ultimately be debugged on one.

The single most characteristic source of mobile bugs is the activity or view-controller lifecycle. The operating system can pause, stop, recreate or destroy the app's screens in response to a phone call, a rotation, or memory pressure, invoking callbacks such as onCreate/onPause/onDestroy on Android or viewDidLoad/viewWillAppear on iOS. Code that assumes a screen stays alive, or that fails to save and restore state across a recreation, produces crashes and lost data that are reproduced by rotating the device or by enabling 'Don't keep activities' in the developer options.

Memory management is the second characteristic front. On Android, retaining a reference to an Activity or Context beyond its lifecycle causes memory leaks that tools like the Android Studio Memory Profiler and the LeakCanary library detect. On iOS, the reference-counting model (ARC) leaks when two objects hold strong references to each other in a retain cycle, diagnosed with the Leaks instrument and the memory graph debugger; the cure is to make one reference weak.

Permissions and configuration produce a class of bugs that never appear in the code's logic. Modern mobile platforms require the user to grant runtime permissions for the camera, location, contacts and so on, and an app that assumes a permission was granted will fail exactly when it is denied. Debugging these means reproducing the denied-permission path deliberately, because on the developer's own device the permission was probably granted long ago and the failing branch is never exercised.

The mobile network deserves special attention because it is intermittent and slow by nature. An app that works on office Wi-Fi may fail on a train with a flaky connection, so mobile debugging includes throttling and disabling the network in the emulator, and inspecting traffic with a proxy such as Charles or mitmproxy or with Android Studio's network profiler. Many mobile bugs are simply the app's poor handling of a timeout, a lost connection or an out-of-order response.

Because so many crashes happen on users' devices in the field, mobile teams rely heavily on remote crash-reporting services such as Firebase Crashlytics or Apple's built-in crash organizer. These collect the stack trace, the device model, the OS version and a breadcrumb trail leading up to each crash and aggregate them, so the developer sees which crashes affect the most users and can debug from a symbolicated report even though the failure happened on a device they will never touch.

Fragmentation across devices and OS versions is a background condition of all mobile debugging. Android in particular runs on thousands of device models with different screen sizes, manufacturers' modifications and OS versions, so a defect may be specific to one vendor or one API level. Testing on a representative matrix of devices — physical and cloud-based device farms — is how these device-specific bugs are found before users encounter them.

Thus, debugging mobile applications extends ordinary code debugging with platform-specific tools and concerns: Android Studio with ADB and Logcat on one side and Xcode with LLDB and Instruments on the other provide breakpoints, logs and profilers, emulators and real devices cover different needs, and a disciplined focus on the lifecycle, memory, permissions, the intermittent network and field crash reports addresses the defects that are unique to running software on constrained, highly varied mobile hardware.
questions: What platform constraints make debugging a mobile application different from debugging a desktop program?
What roles do ADB and Logcat play in debugging an Android application?
What are LLDB and Instruments used for when debugging an iOS application in Xcode?
Why can some bugs be reproduced only on a real device and not on an emulator or simulator?
Why is the activity/view-controller lifecycle such a common source of mobile bugs, and how is a lifecycle defect reproduced?

title: Tools for debugging IoT devices and microcontrollers
outline: Why embedded/IoT debugging differs from debugging on a PC.
Serial output as the universal first diagnostic.
Hardware debugging with JTAG/SWD and in-circuit debuggers.
IoT connectivity, protocols and cloud-side diagnostics.
Constraints: resources, timing and remote deployment.
body: An IoT device or microcontroller is a small computer embedded in a physical product, typically with kilobytes of RAM, no screen, no operating system in the desktop sense, and a processor running directly on the hardware. Debugging such a system is unlike debugging a PC application, because there is often no console to print to, no file system for logs, limited or no ability to attach a conventional debugger, and the added dimension that the software interacts with real electrical signals, sensors and actuators.

The simplest and most universal diagnostic on an embedded device is serial output. The microcontroller sends text over a UART serial line, which a developer reads on a host computer through a serial monitor — the Arduino IDE's Serial Monitor, PuTTY, or 'screen' on Linux. Printing values and progress markers over serial is the embedded equivalent of print-debugging, and on the smallest devices it is frequently the only diagnostic channel available, so laying out clear serial messages is a core skill.

Serial debugging has real limits, however. It changes timing, because sending characters takes milliseconds that can mask or move a timing-dependent bug; it consumes scarce program memory; and it cannot easily inspect state without the developer having anticipated and coded a print for it. For anything beyond basic tracing, embedded developers turn to true hardware debugging through a dedicated debug port on the chip.

That debug port is exposed through standard interfaces, principally JTAG and the two-wire SWD (Serial Wire Debug) used on ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers. A hardware probe — an ST-Link, J-Link, or a debugger built into the development board — connects this port to the host and allows genuine source-level debugging of code running on the physical chip: setting breakpoints, halting the processor, single-stepping and reading registers and memory. This is in-circuit debugging, and it brings desktop-class control to a device with no screen of its own.

On the host side this is orchestrated by familiar software. GDB, the GNU debugger, connects through a bridge such as OpenOCD or a J-Link GDB server to the probe, so the same GDB commands used for a PC program drive the microcontroller. Vendor IDEs — STM32CubeIDE, Microchip's MPLAB X, Espressif's ESP-IDF with its idf.py monitor, and the PlatformIO extension for VS Code — wrap this in a graphical debugger, but underneath, GDB-over-probe is the common mechanism.

Hardware debugging exposes capabilities that pure software cannot. Because the debugger can halt the actual processor, it can read the exact contents of CPU registers and peripheral registers, showing the true state of the hardware — the value latched in a timer, the bits set in a GPIO configuration register — which is often where an embedded bug really lives. Many probes also support hardware breakpoints and watchpoints that trigger when a specific memory address is read or written, invaluable when memory is being corrupted by an unknown culprit.

A distinctive hazard of embedded debugging is that halting the processor at a breakpoint stops the whole physical system, including motors, communications and timers that were mid-operation. Stopping a motor controller at a breakpoint can leave a motor energized; pausing a network stack can drop a connection and desynchronize a protocol. The developer must therefore reason about the physical consequences of a halt, and for timing-critical code use non-intrusive techniques such as trace or toggling a GPIO pin observed on an oscilloscope or logic analyzer instead of stopping.

The 'IoT' dimension adds networking on top of the microcontroller. Devices communicate over Wi-Fi, Bluetooth Low Energy, LoRa, Zigbee or cellular, using lightweight application protocols such as MQTT and CoAP designed for constrained links. Debugging the connectivity means capturing and inspecting these messages — an MQTT client subscribed to the broker's topics, a BLE sniffer, or Wireshark on the network — to confirm whether the device is publishing the data it should and receiving the commands sent to it.

Because an IoT product is usually one node among many talking to a cloud back end, diagnostics extend to the server side. Platforms such as AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub and Google Cloud IoT log device connections, message throughput and errors, and provide device shadows or twins that hold the last known state. When a device 'stops working', these cloud logs often reveal whether it is still connecting and publishing at all, localizing the fault to the device, the link or the back end before any hardware is touched.

Resource constraints shape every embedded debugging decision. With only kilobytes of RAM and a fixed flash size, adding logging or debug instrumentation can itself exhaust memory or overflow the stack, and the compiler's optimizations may reorder or eliminate code so that source-level stepping behaves strangely — which is why debug builds often reduce optimization. Stack overflow, heap fragmentation and running out of flash are themselves common embedded bugs, diagnosed by watching memory usage reports and stack canaries rather than by stepping.

Timing and concurrency defects are especially prominent because embedded code deals directly with interrupts and real-time deadlines. An interrupt service routine that runs while the main loop is mid-update can corrupt shared data exactly like a thread race, and a routine that takes too long can cause missed deadlines or watchdog resets. These are debugged with an oscilloscope or logic analyzer that shows the real timing of signals, and by careful use of atomic access and short interrupt handlers, because a debugger's breakpoint would itself destroy the timing being investigated.

The watchdog timer is a piece of embedded machinery the developer must account for while debugging: a hardware counter that resets the device if the software fails to 'pet' it periodically, guarding against hangs. During a debugging halt the watchdog would normally fire and reset the chip, so debuggers can be configured to freeze the watchdog while the core is stopped; forgetting this leads to the mysterious symptom of a device that keeps rebooting the instant a breakpoint is hit.

Finally, IoT devices are deployed in the field, often in inaccessible locations, so remote and post-deployment diagnostics matter as much as bench debugging. Over-the-air firmware updates deliver fixes without physical access, and remote logging or telemetry sent to the cloud lets developers observe behaviour on devices they cannot reach. Designing a device to report its own health and to be updatable is part of making it debuggable at all once it leaves the workbench.

Thus, debugging IoT devices and microcontrollers layers several techniques onto constrained, physical hardware: serial output gives a universal first trace, JTAG/SWD probes driven by GDB and vendor IDEs bring true source-level and register-level control, protocol capture and cloud-side logs diagnose the networked dimension, and a constant awareness of limited resources, real-time timing, the watchdog and remote deployment governs which of these techniques can safely be used on any given problem.
questions: Why is debugging a microcontroller fundamentally different from debugging a desktop application?
What is serial-output debugging, and what are its main limitations on an embedded device?
What do JTAG/SWD interfaces and a hardware debug probe make possible that serial output cannot?
Why can halting the processor at a breakpoint be hazardous on an embedded or real-time system, and what alternatives exist?
How do protocol capture and cloud-side logs help localize a fault in a networked IoT device?

title: Debugging code in an integrated development environment for embedded systems
outline: The role of the embedded IDE and its integrated toolchain.
The build-flash-debug cycle and the debug session.
Peripheral, register and memory views specific to embedded work.
Real-time and RTOS-aware debugging features.
Simulation, HAL/board-support layers and configuration tools.
body: An integrated development environment for embedded systems brings together, in one application, everything needed to turn source code into a program running on a microcontroller and to debug it there: an editor, a cross-compiler toolchain, a flashing utility and a debugger that talks to a hardware probe. Examples include STM32CubeIDE for STMicroelectronics chips, MPLAB X for Microchip devices, MCUXpresso for NXP, Espressif's ESP-IDF, and the increasingly popular combination of VS Code with the PlatformIO or Cortex-Debug extensions. Unlike a desktop IDE, an embedded IDE must manage the fact that the code is compiled on one machine and executed on another.

This cross-development model is the central concept. The host computer runs a cross-compiler — a compiler that produces machine code for the target processor rather than for the host — typically the GNU Arm Embedded toolchain (arm-none-eabi-gcc) for ARM devices. The IDE orchestrates this compiler, the linker with its memory-map linker script, and the tools that convert the result into a flashable image, so that a single click builds an executable for a processor entirely different from the developer's PC.

Debugging in an embedded IDE follows a build-flash-debug cycle. The IDE compiles the code, downloads (flashes) the resulting image into the microcontroller's non-volatile memory through the debug probe, resets the chip and halts it at the entry point, then hands control to the developer with the program stopped and ready to step. Understanding that flashing physically rewrites the device's program memory — and that it takes time and has limited write endurance — distinguishes this cycle from simply running a program on a PC.

Once the session is live, the IDE presents the familiar debugging surface: breakpoints in the source, single-stepping, a call stack and local-variable views, and expression evaluation, all driven under the hood by GDB communicating with a probe server such as OpenOCD or ST-Link GDB Server. The developer sets a breakpoint in an interrupt handler or a setup routine and steps through it while the code executes on the real chip, seeing the same source-level view they would expect on a desktop debugger.

What sets the embedded IDE apart is its hardware-aware views. A peripheral or SFR (special-function-register) view decodes the microcontroller's memory-mapped registers into named fields, so instead of reading a raw hexadecimal word the developer sees, for example, that the UART's transmit-enable bit is set and its baud-rate divisor holds a particular value. These views are generated from the chip vendor's description files (such as CMSIS SVD files) and are indispensable, because most embedded bugs come down to a peripheral configured incorrectly.

Alongside peripheral decoding, the IDE offers raw register and memory windows. The core-register view shows the CPU's general-purpose registers, the program counter, the stack pointer and the status flags; the memory view shows any region of RAM or flash as bytes or words that can be examined and edited live. Because embedded code manipulates hardware and memory directly, being able to read the exact stack pointer or watch a specific RAM address change is often the only way to catch memory corruption or a misbehaving pointer.
