Watchpoints, also called data breakpoints, are a hardware feature the IDE exposes and that matters greatly in embedded work. Rather than stopping at a line of code, a watchpoint halts the processor when a chosen memory address is read or written, so the developer can catch the exact instant a variable is unexpectedly overwritten. Because they use the chip's built-in debug hardware, only a small fixed number are available, but they are the fastest route to finding what is corrupting a value.

Because timing is critical, modern embedded IDEs support tracing, which records program flow with minimal disruption instead of halting. ARM's ITM and SWO facility lets the chip stream printf-style messages and event markers over a single pin to the host at high speed, and the ETM trace records the exact sequence of executed instructions. Trace lets the developer reconstruct what happened in a time-critical routine after the fact, without the breakpoint-induced timing distortion that would hide the very bug being sought.

When the firmware runs on a real-time operating system such as FreeRTOS or Zephyr, the IDE offers RTOS-aware debugging. Instead of showing a single confusing stack, the debugger enumerates all the RTOS tasks, showing each task's state (running, ready, blocked), its own stack and its priority. This turns an opaque multitasking system into an intelligible list of tasks, letting the developer see which task is blocked, which is starving and how much of each task's stack is used — the embedded analogue of a thread window.

Many embedded IDEs also provide an instruction-set simulator that can run the compiled firmware on the host without any hardware. Simulation is valuable early in development, for continuous-integration testing, and for debugging pure algorithmic logic, because it is fast, repeatable and imposes no risk to hardware. Its limitation is that it cannot faithfully model real sensors, real timing and real peripherals, so hardware bugs must eventually be reproduced on the actual chip.

The layers of software the IDE generates shape how debugging is done. Vendors ship a hardware-abstraction layer (HAL) and board-support package that wrap the raw registers in higher-level functions, and graphical configuration tools such as STM32CubeMX or MPLAB Harmony generate initialization code from a pin-and-clock diagram. A great many embedded bugs are actually misconfigurations produced or left out at this layer, so debugging frequently means stepping into the generated initialization code and comparing the resulting register values, in the peripheral view, against what the datasheet requires.

Configuration for debugging itself must be correct or the session misleads the developer. The build must be a debug build with optimization reduced, because aggressive optimization reorders and eliminates code so that stepping jumps unpredictably and variables read as 'optimized out'; the debug symbols must match the exact image flashed to the device; and the probe's connection settings and clock speed must suit the target. Mismatched symbols or an over-optimized build are a common reason a debug session shows nonsensical values.

Because embedded resources are tiny, the IDE's build reports are themselves a diagnostic tool. The map file and the memory-usage summary show how much flash and RAM the program consumes and how close it is to the limits, and stack-analysis features estimate worst-case stack depth. Watching these figures catches a whole class of failures — running out of flash, overflowing the stack, fragmenting the heap — that never appear as an exception but as sudden, unexplained resets or corruption.

Thus, an embedded IDE turns a bare microcontroller into something that can be debugged with near-desktop convenience by integrating a cross-compiler toolchain with a probe-driven debugger and, crucially, by adding hardware-aware capabilities — peripheral and register decoding, memory watchpoints, low-intrusion trace, RTOS task views, simulation and configuration tools — that address the peripheral, timing and resource faults which dominate embedded development and which a general-purpose debugger cannot see.
questions: What does an embedded IDE integrate, and why is cross-compilation central to embedded development?
Describe the build-flash-debug cycle and how it differs from running a program on a desktop computer.
Why is a peripheral/special-function-register view so important when debugging embedded code?
What is a watchpoint (data breakpoint), and why are only a limited number available on a microcontroller?
What does RTOS-aware debugging add over an ordinary debugger, and when is an instruction-set simulator useful?

title: Debugging code at the level of system programming
outline: What system programming is and why its bugs are harder.
System calls, the kernel boundary and strace/ltrace/dtrace.
Memory errors and the tools that detect them.
Analyzing crashes: core dumps, signals and post-mortem debugging.
Kernel- and driver-level debugging.
body: System programming is the writing of software that operates close to the hardware and the operating system: kernels, device drivers, system libraries, run-time environments, and services that manage memory, processes, files and devices directly. Written predominantly in C, C++ and increasingly Rust, this code has little or no safety net — an error does not politely raise an exception but can corrupt memory, crash the whole machine or silently produce wrong results — which makes debugging at this level demanding and precise.

A defining feature of system programming is direct manual control over memory. There is no garbage collector; the programmer allocates and frees memory explicitly, works with raw pointers and pointer arithmetic, and manages buffers by hand. Consequently the most common and dangerous defects are memory errors: buffer overflows that write past the end of an array, use-after-free where memory is accessed after being released, double frees, uninitialized reads, and memory leaks. These often do not crash at the point of the mistake but corrupt data that causes a failure much later and far away.

The boundary between a program and the operating system is the system call — the controlled entry into the kernel to request a service such as opening a file, reading from a socket or allocating pages. Much system-programming behaviour, and many bugs, live at this boundary, so a key diagnostic technique is to observe the sequence of system calls a program makes. On Linux 'strace' traces system calls and their arguments and return values, 'ltrace' traces library calls, and the more powerful DTrace and eBPF/bpftrace instrument the running kernel and applications with minimal overhead.

Reading an strace transcript answers questions the source cannot. It shows exactly which files a program tried to open and whether the call returned an error such as ENOENT or EACCES, which network addresses it connected to, and where it blocked waiting for input. A program that 'hangs' or 'cannot find its configuration' is frequently diagnosed in seconds by strace revealing a failed open on an unexpected path or a read that never returns, without ever opening the source code.

The classic interactive debugger for system-level code is GDB, and on macOS and LLVM-based toolchains its counterpart LLDB. Beyond ordinary breakpoints and stepping, these debuggers let the system programmer examine raw memory, disassemble to the instruction level, inspect and modify CPU registers, and set hardware watchpoints on a memory address. When a bug lives in pointer arithmetic or a corrupted structure, the ability to read memory byte by byte and to step one machine instruction at a time is what makes it findable.

Because memory errors are so pervasive and so hard to catch by inspection, specialized detectors are essential. Valgrind's Memcheck runs the program on a synthetic CPU and reports every invalid read or write, use of uninitialized memory and leak, at the cost of a large slowdown. The compiler-based sanitizers — AddressSanitizer for out-of-bounds and use-after-free, UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer for undefined behaviour, and ThreadSanitizer for data races — instrument the code at build time and catch errors at the exact moment they occur, turning silent corruption into an immediate, located report.

When a system program crashes, the operating system can be configured to write a core dump: a snapshot of the process's entire memory, registers and stack at the instant of the fault. This enables post-mortem debugging — loading the core dump into GDB later ('gdb program core') to examine a backtrace and the values of variables as they were at the crash, even though the program is no longer running. Core-dump analysis is often the only way to debug a crash that occurred on a server in production and cannot be reproduced on demand.

Unix-like systems deliver faults and events to a program as signals, and understanding them is part of system-level debugging. A segmentation fault arrives as SIGSEGV when the program touches memory it may not, an illegal instruction as SIGILL, and an abort from a failed assertion as SIGABRT. Knowing that a crash was a SIGSEGV immediately tells the developer the fault is a bad memory access, and a debugger stopped on the signal shows the exact instruction and address involved, pointing straight at the offending pointer.

System programs are also frequently multithreaded and interact through shared memory, pipes and sockets, so they suffer the full range of concurrency defects — data races and deadlocks — but without the higher-level protections of managed languages. ThreadSanitizer detects races by instrumenting memory accesses, GDB can inspect and switch between all threads of a stopped process, and helgrind (part of Valgrind) analyzes locking. Because these bugs are timing-dependent, the same nondeterminism and 'heisenbug' behaviour described for concurrency in general applies with full force here.

The deepest level is debugging the operating-system kernel and device drivers themselves, where a bug can freeze or crash the entire machine and where an ordinary debugger running as a user process cannot reach. On Linux, tools include printk logging read through dmesg, the in-tree KGDB and KDB kernel debuggers, and crash-dump analysis of a kernel panic with the 'crash' utility on a vmcore captured by kdump. A kernel oops or panic message, with its register dump and call trace, is the primary evidence, much as a user-space stack trace is.

Kernel debugging is typically done across two machines or through virtualization, because the machine being debugged may halt entirely. A common arrangement connects a debugger on a host to a target kernel over a serial line or network, or runs the kernel inside a virtual machine such as QEMU whose built-in GDB stub lets the host set breakpoints in kernel code. Windows uses an analogous model with WinDbg connected to a target over a debug transport. This host/target separation mirrors the embedded debugging seen earlier, for the same reason: you cannot reliably debug a system from inside the very system that has stopped.

System-level debugging also demands attention to the build and the environment. Optimized release builds inline functions and discard frame pointers, so backtraces become incomplete and variables read as 'optimized out'; therefore debug builds with symbols and reduced optimization, or separately shipped symbol files, are used for diagnosis. Undefined behaviour in C and C++ means the same source may behave differently across compilers and optimization levels, so a bug that appears only in the optimized build is a signal to look for undefined behaviour, checked with the sanitizers.

Because reproducibility is scarce at this level — races, corruption and hardware interactions may appear only intermittently — system programmers lean on defensive and evidence-gathering techniques: liberal assertions that fail fast at the first sign of an invalid state, guard pages and canaries that turn silent overruns into immediate faults, deterministic replay and record-and-replay debuggers such as rr that let a nondeterministic failure be replayed exactly, and thorough logging through the system journal. These raise the probability that a rare fault leaves enough evidence, or can be re-run, to be understood.

Thus, debugging at the level of system programming is the most exacting form of debugging: with no managed run-time to catch mistakes, the engineer relies on system-call tracing to observe the kernel boundary, memory sanitizers and Valgrind to expose the pervasive pointer and memory errors, core dumps and signals to reconstruct crashes after the fact, and specialized kernel and record-replay tools to reach faults that ordinary debuggers and single machines cannot — always compensating for the absence of safety with discipline, assertions and careful control of the build.
questions: What is system programming, and why do its bugs tend to be more dangerous than those in managed, high-level languages?
How do strace and ltrace help diagnose a system program's interaction with the operating system?
What kinds of memory errors are common in system programming, and how do Valgrind and the sanitizers detect them?
What is a core dump, and how does it enable post-mortem debugging of a crash that cannot be reproduced?
Why is kernel and driver debugging usually performed across two machines or through virtualization?
